Not a bad first move I must say. As this is a teaching game I'll make comments here and you can respond using the message box also.

In the opening moves players usually play to stake out territory. You've started be placing a very safe stone in the upper corner on the 2/3 lines (C8). I've placed my stone on the 3/4 line (G4 - a more common opening move). The reason for placing it slightly higher is to try and claim more territory. For example ""underneath"" your stone are about 5 points (A9, B9, C9, A8 and B8) whereas with the 3/4 stone there are 11. In a 9x9 game like this one another common starting point is the centre E5 - though that does invite a lot of fighting.
Your next move would usually be in either of the other corners (F7 or G7) or (C3 or D4). You could also attack my stone by placing your next move at F3.

So if you can guestimate the value of another move to be greater then that's the tack you follow. Otherwise you can save the corner. C[tzorec: OK. This is a good opportunity to discuss eyes (that is one or more intersections surrounded by your stones). As I've probably already said there are two types of eyes -  'false' and 'real'. The difference between false and real eyes is one thing - connection. In our game Black has 4 groups - the collection of stones around C6, the single stone G7, the other single stone J5, and the stones G3, F2, G1 and H2. The two single stones obviously can't be eyes. The stones around C6 already have 2 'real' eyes - they are made up of the stones C6, C7, D7, E7, E6 & D5 and B3, A3, A4, B5 & C4. The stones in the lower right have one eye - a false one. As can be seen there are no connecting stones. The stones in this corner suffer a further problem ... I occupy the vital intersections needed to complete a connection and make an eye around G2 - that is I occupy both F3 and H3 of which you would need at least one to have any chance of making 2 eyes. As such they are doomed ... unless you can capture something - but my next move removes those threats by connecting my stones together.

C[tzorec: Remember to check liberties when placing a stone (or ""breathing spaces"" - that is the number of unoccupied intersections remaining). The last move D9 put the black stone into ""self atari"" - it only had 1 liberty remaining after being placed - which meant White was able to take it immediately - which I've done. Have a look with 'Analyse Board'.

Let me know.

C[tzorec: Good move. I'm going to try and keep the corner. If you can stop me from making 2 eyes then you will capture all my pieces and get the corner.

tzorec: An Eye is a series of connected stones with at least one unoccupied intersection on the inside. So if you had stones at E7, F7, G7, E6, G6, E5, F5 and G5 then you would have one eye. For any pieces to stay on the board and not be captured then they must be connected to two eyes. I would highly recommend checking out - http://senseis.xmp.net/?PagesForBeginners as an intro.
My move is threatening to capture your stone at D4. How can you save that stone?
To make a safe group (one that cannot be captured) you need two connected eyes:

The reason it cannot be captured is because if the suicide rule:  you cannot play a move such that the stone just placed has no liberties, but all opposing stones still have.Â Suicides are forbidden - except under special circumstances.
To capture a piece, or pieces that are connected, the opposing player must occupy all the intersections immediately adjacent to the stone (or stones):
So in the above diagram White has captured Black. After taking the captured piece off the board the result looks like:
Black now cannot play straight back in the middle of the White stones because that would be suicide.

C[tzorec: Hello. The reason you can't play G3 and take my stone is because of the ""Ko"" rule. That rule happens when there is a possible ever repeating situation (like G3/G2 in the game) - the rule states that once I took your stone with G2 you must play somewhere else on the board first before you can play G3. This allows me (as I have done) to fill in G3 and end the Ko. Otherwise games with this situation may never end. As to your second question ""have i created any eye's"" the answer is not yet. This is one reason why the Ko rule is important. If you could have retaken G3 (before your last play of H1) and filled it in you might have been able to make two eyes (you'd also need to fill in J2). C[tzorec: ""Seki"" is a situation whereby both players are unable to make a move against the other player without being captured themselves. In our game had Black played B1 then White could not play A1 because Black would play B3 and capture all the White stones AND similarly if (after Black B1) Black played A1 then White would play B3 and capture all the Black stones.

With scoring where there is a seki then none of the area, nor the stones, count towards either player. Effectively, all the territory and all the stones are neutralized. Creating a seki can be very useful to cancel out an opponent's territory.

C[tzorec: Good. Now we're in the end game and at this stage you look over your area and groups and decide if you can expand them or check that none of your groups are in danger of being captured. After that check you look to your opponent's area and groups and decide if you can reduce or capture any of theirs. Similarly, you can look for invasion points where you might be able to jump into your opponent's area and create a living group that will further reduce their area (a 3-3 invasion is a common example).

C[tzorec: Q12 is a nice solid connection ... but P12 is a ""lighter"" and more effective connection.
tzorec: With P12 you create 2 ""lion's mouth"" connections (you can see the lion's mouth at P13 and Q12 - there's also another at E3) :^)
tzorec: The risk involved with the solid connection is that I can jump in at P13 and try and cut your stones off from the centre.
tzorec: From the variation you can see that your side stones are now cut from the centre and it leaves them in a state where they might be captured.
tzorec: This is by no means the only way things might go but one possibility.
tzorec: Whereas playing move 65 at P12 eliminates that risk and keeps your stones connected to the centre (where if necessary you could join up with other stones or make a second eye :^)
tzorec: Just let me know if you want an undo :^)

tzorec: So looking over the board - 1: Are any of my groups in danger (or can they be attacked)? 2: Are any of my opponent's groups in danger (or can they be attacked)? and 3: Where is the next BIGGEST point for me to play?
tzorec: Your move P12 effectively eliminates point 2 (I can't effectively attack that group any more as the are too well connected to the centre with a foot on the side :^)
tzorec: As to point 1 - my side group is still a little unsettled and although my group around E17 is strong it still has no eyes (though it shouldn't be too hard to make 2
tzorec: As to point 3 the two main areas are the centre and the lower side.

tzorec: So a good move is one that does more than one thing ... so I'm going to invade the lower side. This will help my floating right side group, hopefully take away some of your lower territory and make a springboard to attack the centre (where you have a lot of influence from M13, N10 and the strong wall at D4)

C[tzorec: I could have also tried to immediately invade the centre with something like M7. I would have chosen that point because it's right in the middle of a line drawn on your ""boarder"" between N10 and K4 which is the best starting point to reduce a large territory.
